---
date: "2026-06-22"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

## The Shape, and the Theorems Ahead

- Part 11 — the bell shape and the theorems ahead

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter one-point-three, part eleven of eleven, the finale. In part ten we built the sampling distribution by simulation and confirmed its center and spread. Now we look at the one thing the formulas cannot give us — the shape — and watch it transform as the sample grows. Then we point ahead to the two great limit theorems that explain everything we have seen, and send Priya off with a better sticky note.
:::

## What the Histograms Reveal

- Plot for $N=10$, $50$, $500$ side by side
- All centered on one line at $\mu$ — $N$ doesn't move it
- Histograms narrow at the $1/\sqrt{N}$ rate — *this is consistency*
- $N=10$ still right-skewed; $N=500$ a clean bell
- Why does the bell appear? Not yet earned

::: notes
Now the part you cannot get from the formulas — the shape, and how it changes with N. We plot the sampling distribution for sample sizes of ten, fifty, and five hundred, side by side. Three things jump out, and they are the takeaways of the chapter. First, all three histograms are centered on the same line at mu, eight thousand dollars. Growing N does not move the center, because unbiasedness holds at every N — the recipe always aims true. Second, the histograms get dramatically narrower as N grows, and they narrow at the one-over-root-N rate: going from ten to five hundred is a fiftyfold jump in data, so the standard error should fall by the square root of fifty, about sevenfold — and it does. This visible collapse onto the line is consistency; you are watching x bar converge in probability to mu happen before your eyes. Third, the shape changes too. At N equals ten, drawing from a skewed loss distribution, the histogram of x bar is still noticeably right-skewed — it remembers the shape of the underlying claims. By N equals five hundred it has become a clean, symmetric bell, no matter that the individual claims are anything but bell-shaped. That is not an accident, and it is not something we have earned yet.
:::

## Where This Is Heading

- We *observed* two facts; we have not *explained* them
- **Law of Large Numbers** explains the concentration on $\mu$
- **Central Limit Theorem** explains the bell shape
- Both are the whole of Chapter 1.4
- Priya's better note: "$\$8{,}410$, standard error about $\$850$"

::: notes
Step back and notice what we did and did not do. We defined the sampling distribution and observed, by simulation, two stunning facts: as N grows it concentrates on the truth, and it becomes normal. We have not yet explained either one. That is deliberate. The two explanations are the two great limit theorems of statistics. The Law of Large Numbers explains the concentration — why the distribution piles up on mu, the rigorous engine behind the word consistency. The Central Limit Theorem explains the bell shape — why the sampling distribution of x bar is approximately normal for large N even when the data are skewed. Both are the entire subject of Chapter one-point-four, where the sigma-over-root-N standard error stops being a formula you trust and becomes the thing you build confidence intervals and, later, hypothesis tests out of. Priya now writes a better sticky note. Not eight thousand four hundred ten, but eight thousand four hundred ten, standard error about eight hundred fifty dollars. The first is a rumor; the second is an estimate that knows how much to trust itself.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The sampling distribution stays centered on $\mu$, narrows at the $1/\sqrt{N}$ rate (consistency), and morphs from skewed to a clean **bell** as $N$ grows. We *observed* this; the **Law of Large Numbers** and **Central Limit Theorem** *explain* it — the whole of Chapter 1.4. You now own the chapter's vocabulary: estimator, estimate, bias, variance, MSE, consistency, sampling distribution.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part eleven and the whole chapter: the sampling distribution stays centered on the true mean, confirming unbiasedness; narrows at the one-over-root-N rate, which is consistency in action; and morphs from skewed to a clean bell as the sample grows. We observed both the concentration and the bell, but explained neither. The two explanations are the great limit theorems — the Law of Large Numbers for the concentration, the Central Limit Theorem for the bell — and they are the entire subject of the next chapter, Chapter one-point-four. You now own the vocabulary that makes that chapter possible: estimator versus estimate, bias, variance, mean squared error and its decomposition, consistency, and the sampling distribution that ties them all together. Priya leaves with a sticky note that knows how much to trust itself, and you leave ready to prove why it can.
:::
